title: Operators & Math
subtitle: Make Java do your homework: add, multiply, and unleash the Math superpowers.
goal: Use arithmetic operators, understand integer vs double division, master precedence, ++ and += shortcuts, casting, and the built-in Math class.

blocks.heading: Java is a giant calculator 🔢
blocks.html:
Deep down, every computer loves doing math. Java gives you five basic operators to crunch numbers:

  • + add
  • - subtract
  • * multiply (we use a star, not an x)
  • / divide
  • % remainder (called modulo) — what's left over after dividing

Let's put them to work.

blocks.caption: The five basic operators
blocks.explain: Notice 7 / 3 gave 2, not 2.33! And 7 % 3 gave 1, because 3 goes into 7 twice with 1 left over. The % operator is great for asking 'is this number even?' or 'what's the leftover?'.

blocks.heading: The sneaky division trap 🪤
blocks.html:
Here's a gotcha that catches almost everyone. When you divide two whole numbers (ints), Java throws away the decimal part and keeps only the whole number. So 5 / 2 is 2, not 2.5!

To get the real answer, at least one number must be a double (a number with a decimal point). Adding .0 does the trick.

blocks.caption: Integer division vs double division
blocks.explain: 5 / 2 is integer division, so Java chops off the .5 and gives 2. But 5.0 / 2 has a double in it, so Java keeps the full 2.5. One decimal point changes everything!

blocks.html: Watch out! 5 / 2 is 2, but 5.0 / 2 is 2.5. If your math answer is missing its decimals, check whether you accidentally divided two whole numbers.

blocks.heading: Order of operations (PEMDAS lives!) ➗
blocks.html: Just like in math class, Java does * and / before + and -. If you want a different order, wrap it in parentheses ( ) — whatever is inside happens first.

blocks.caption: Parentheses change the answer
blocks.explain: In the first line, 3 * 4 happens first (= 12), then + 2 gives 14. In the second line, the parentheses force 2 + 3 first (= 5), then * 4 gives 20.

blocks.question: What does 9 / 4 print in Java when both are whole numbers (ints)?
blocks.explain: Integer division throws away the decimal part. 4 goes into 9 twice with leftovers, so the answer is 2. (If you wrote 9.0 / 4 you'd get 2.25.)

blocks.question: What is the value of 2 + 3 * 4 in Java?
blocks.explain: Multiplication happens before addition, so 3 * 4 = 12 first, then + 2 = 14. Use parentheses if you want the addition first.

blocks.heading: Shortcuts: ++, --, and += 🚀
blocks.html:
Programmers are lazy in a good way. Instead of writing score = score + 1, you can write:

  • score++ — add 1 (increment)
  • score-- — subtract 1 (decrement)
  • score += 10 — add 10 (same as score = score + 10)
  • score -= 2, score *= 3, score /= 2 — and so on

blocks.caption: Compound assignment in action
blocks.explain: score++ bumped 5 to 6. Then += 10 made it 16. Then *= 2 doubled it to 32. These shortcuts save typing and are everywhere in real code, especially in loops.

blocks.heading: Casting: changing a number's type 🎭
blocks.html: Sometimes you have a double like 3.9 but you want a plain int. You can cast it by putting the type in parentheses in front: (int) 3.9. Casting a double to an int just chops off the decimals — it does NOT round!

blocks.caption: Casting chops off the decimal
blocks.explain: (int) 3.9 becomes 3 — Java drops the .9, it does NOT round up to 4. If you actually want rounding, use Math.round() which you'll meet next.

blocks.heading: The Math class: built-in superpowers 🦸
blocks.html:
Java comes with a toolbox called Math full of handy helpers:

  • Math.max(a, b) and Math.min(a, b) — biggest / smallest
  • Math.abs(x) — absolute value (drops the minus sign)
  • Math.pow(a, b) — a to the power of b
  • Math.sqrt(x) — square root
  • Math.round(x) — round to the nearest whole number
  • Math.random() — a random number between 0 and 1

blocks.caption: Math class helpers
blocks.explain: Math.pow and Math.sqrt give doubles (notice 32.0 and 9.0). Math.round(3.6) rounds UP to 4 — unlike casting, which would have chopped it to 3. Math.abs(-7) turned -7 into 7.

blocks.html: Rolling dice 🎲: Math.random() gives a decimal from 0 up to (but not including) 1. To get a dice roll 1–6, use (int)(Math.random() * 6) + 1. The cast chops it to a whole number, and + 1 shifts it from 0–5 to 1–6.

blocks.caption: A random dice roll (run it a few times!)
blocks.explain: Each time you run this you get a different number from 1 to 6, because Math.random() picks a fresh random value. We don't show fixed output here since it changes every run!

blocks.question: What does Math.round(3.6) return?
blocks.explain: Math.round() rounds to the NEAREST whole number, so 3.6 becomes 4. (Casting with (int) would have chopped it down to 3 instead.)

blocks.title: Fix the division
blocks.instructions: This program tries to split 7 cookies among 2 friends and show the exact amount each gets. Right now it prints 3 (wrong!). Change it so it prints 3.5 by making the division use doubles.
blocks.passMsg: Nailed it! One little .0 unlocked the real answer. 🍪
blocks.failMsg: Make at least one number a double — try writing 7.0 / 2 so Java keeps the decimal.

blocks.title: Find the bigger number's square root
blocks.instructions: Use Math.max to pick the bigger of 36 and 49, then print its square root with Math.sqrt. The answer should be 7.0.
blocks.passMsg: Superb — you nested two Math powers together! 🦸
blocks.failMsg: Use Math.max(36, 49) to get 49, then wrap it in Math.sqrt(...) to get 7.0.

challenge.title: Build a tip calculator
challenge.instructions: A meal costs 50 dollars and you want to leave a 20% tip. Calculate the tip (use doubles so you keep the decimals!), then print the total bill. The total should be 60.0. Hint: tip = bill * 20 / 100, and total = bill + tip.
challenge.hint: The tip is bill * 20 / 100. Because bill is a double, the whole calculation stays a double. Then add it to bill to get the total.
challenge.passMsg: 🏆 You built a real tip calculator! You've finished Day 4 — operators and Math are yours now.
